title: Attention Is All You Need
abstract: The dominant sequence transduction models are based on complex recurrent or convolutional neural networks that include an encoder and a decoder. The best performing models also connect the encoder and decoder through an attention mechanism. We propose a new simple network architecture, the Transformer, based solely on attention mechanisms, dispensing with recurrence and convolutions entirely. Experiments on two machine translation tasks show these models to be superior in quality while being more parallelizable and requiring significantly less time to train. Our model achieves 28.4 BLEU on the WMT 2014 English-to-German translation task, improving over the existing best results, including ensembles, by over 2 BLEU. On the WMT 2014 English-to-French translation task, our model establishes a new single-model state-of-the-art BLEU score of 41.8.
authors: Ashish Vaswani, Noam Shazeer, Niki Parmar, Jakob Uszkoreit, Llion Jones, Aidan N. Gomez, Lukasz Kaiser, Illia Polosukhin

title: BERT: Pre-training of Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding
abstract: We introduce a new language representation model called BERT, which stands for Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers. Unlike recent language representation models, BERT is designed to pre-train deep bidirectional representations from unlabeled text by jointly conditioning on both left and right context in all layers. As a result, the pre-trained BERT model can be fine-tuned with just one additional output layer to create state-of-the-art models for a wide range of tasks, such as question answering and language inference, without substantial task-specific architecture modifications. BERT is conceptually simple and empirically powerful. It obtains new state-of-the-art results on eleven natural language processing tasks, including pushing the GLUE score to 80.5%, MultiNLI accuracy to 86.7%, and SQuAD v1.1 question answering F1 to 93.2%.
authors: Jacob Devlin, Ming-Wei Chang, Kenton Lee, Kristina Toutanova

title: Language Models are Few-Shot Learners
abstract: We demonstrate that scaling up language models greatly improves few-shot performance, sometimes even reaching competitiveness with prior state-of-the-art fine-tuning approaches. Specifically, we train GPT-3, an autoregressive language model with 175 billion parameters, 10x more than any previous non-sparse language model, and evaluate its performance in the few-shot setting. For all tasks, GPT-3 is applied without any gradient updates or fine-tuning, with tasks and few-shot demonstrations specified purely via text interaction with the model. GPT-3 achieves strong performance on many NLP datasets, including translation, question-answering, and cloze tasks, as well as several tasks that require on-the-fly reasoning or domain adaptation, such as unscrambling words, using a novel word in a sentence, or performing 3-digit arithmetic.
authors: Tom B. Brown, Benjamin Mann, Nick Ryder, Melanie Subbiah, Jared Kaplan, Prafulla Dhariwal, Arvind Neelakantan, Pranav Shyam, Girish Sastry, Amanda Askell, Sandhini Agarwal, Ariel Herbert-Voss, Gretchen Krueger, Tom Henighan, Rewon Child, Aditya Ramesh, Daniel M. Ziegler, Jeffrey Wu, Clemens Winter, Christopher Hesse, Mark Chen, Eric Sigler, Mateusz Litwin, Scott Gray, Benjamin Chess, Jack Clark, Christopher Berner, Sam McCandlish, Alec Radford, Ilya Sutskever, Dario Amodei

title: Chain-of-Thought Prompting Elicits Reasoning in Large Language Models
abstract: We explore how generating a chain of thought -- a series of intermediate reasoning steps -- significantly improves the ability of large language models to perform complex reasoning. In particular, we show how such reasoning abilities emerge naturally in sufficiently large language models when prompted with a few chain-of-thought demonstrations. We evaluate chain-of-thought prompting on arithmetic, commonsense, and symbolic reasoning benchmarks. On GSM8K, a benchmark of grade school math word problems, chain-of-thought prompting combined with PaLM 540B achieves state-of-the-art performance, outperforming even fine-tuned GPT-3 with verifiers. We also demonstrate that chain-of-thought prompting generalizes to out-of-distribution reasoning problems, showing its versatility in general problem solving.
authors: Jason Wei, Xuezhi Wang, Dale Schuurmans, Maarten Bosma, Brian Ichter, Fei Xia, Ed H. Chi, Quoc V. Le, Denny Zhou

title: Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks
abstract: Large pre-trained language models have been shown to store implicit knowledge in their parameters. However, their ability to access and precisely manipulate knowledge is still limited, and they can struggle on knowledge-intensive tasks. We introduce Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) -- a general-purpose fine-tuning recipe that combines pre-trained parametric and non-parametric memory. Our parametric memory is a pre-trained seq2seq model, and our non-parametric memory is a dense vector index of Wikipedia, accessed with a pre-trained neural retriever. We compare two formulations of RAG: one that retrieves the same passages for the entire generated sequence, and one that can retrieve different passages for each token. We show that RAG models achieve state-of-the-art results on open-domain QA and outperform standard seq2seq models.
authors: Patrick Lewis, Ethan Perez, Aleksandara Piktus, Fabio Petroni, Vladimir Karpukhin, Naman Goyal, Heinrich Küttler, Mike Lewis, Wen-tau Yih, Tim Rocktäschel, Sebastian Riedel, Douwe Kiela

title: An Image is Worth 16x16 Words: Transformers for Image Recognition at Scale
abstract: While the Transformer architecture has become the de-facto standard for natural language processing tasks, its applications to computer vision remain limited. In vision, attention is either applied in conjunction with convolutional networks, or used to replace certain components of convolutional networks while keeping their overall structure in place. We show that this reliance on CNNs is not necessary and a pure transformer applied directly to sequences of image patches can perform very well on image classification tasks. When pre-trained on large amounts of data and transferred to multiple mid-sized or small image recognition benchmarks, the Vision Transformer (ViT) achieves excellent results compared to state-of-the-art convolutional networks while requiring substantially fewer computational resources to train.
authors: Alexey Dosovitskiy, Lucas Beyer, Alexander Kolesnikov, Dirk Weissenborn, Xiaohua Zhai, Thomas Unterthiner, Mostafa Dehghani, Matthias Minderer, Georg Heigold, Sylvain Gelly, Jakob Uszkoreit, Neil Houlsby

title: Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models
abstract: We present high quality image synthesis results using diffusion probabilistic models, a class of latent variable models inspired by considerations from non-equilibrium thermodynamics. Our best results are obtained by training on a weighted variational bound designed according to a novel connection between diffusion models and denoising score matching with Langevin dynamics. Our models naturally admit a progressive lossy-to-lossless decompression scheme. On the unconditional CIFAR-10 dataset, we obtain an Inception score of 9.46 and a state-of-the-art FID score of 3.17. On 256x256 LSUN, we obtain sample quality similar to ProgressiveGAN. We also show that diffusion models are capable of generating extremely realistic human faces and complex indoor scenes.
authors: Jonathan Ho, Ajay Jain, Pieter Abbeel

title: High-Resolution Image Synthesis with Latent Diffusion Models
abstract: By decomposing the image-generation process into a sequential application of denoising autoencoders, diffusion models (DMs) achieve state-of-the-art synthesis results on image data. Furthermore, their formulation allows for a guiding mechanism to control the image generation process without retraining. However, operating directly in the high-dimensional pixel space requires significant computational resources. We propose Latent Diffusion Models (LDMs), which apply diffusion processes in the latent space of powerful pre-trained autoencoders. By introducing cross-attention layers into the model architecture, we turn DMs into general generators for diverse sequential inputs like text or bounding boxes. LDMs achieve state-of-the-art results on image inpainting, class-conditional synthesis, and text-to-image synthesis while drastically reducing GPU hour requirements.
authors: Robin Rombach, Andreas Blattmann, Dominik Lorenz, Patrick Esser, Björn Ommer

title: Learning Transferable Visual Models From Natural Language Supervision
abstract: State-of-the-art computer vision systems are trained to predict a fixed set of predetermined object categories. This restricted form of supervision limits their generality and usability. We show that pre-training on the auxiliary task of predicting which caption goes with which image is an efficient and scalable way to learn SOTA image representations from scratch on a dataset of 400 million (image, text) pairs collected from the internet. After pre-training, natural language is used to reference learned visual concepts, enabling zero-shot transfer of the model to downstream OCR, action recognition, and object classification tasks. Our model, CLIP, matches the performance of a ResNet-50 on ImageNet zero-shot without using any of the 1.28 million labeled examples, demonstrating high transferability.
authors: Alec Radford, Jong Wook Kim, Chris Hallacy, Aditya Ramesh, Gabriel Goh, Sandhini Agarwal, Girish Sastry, Amanda Askell, Pamela Mishkin, Jack Clark, Gretchen Krueger, Ilya Sutskever

title: GPT-4 Technical Report
abstract: We report the development of GPT-4, a large-scale, multimodal model which can accept image and text inputs and produce text outputs. While less capable than humans in many real-world scenarios, GPT-4 exhibits human-level performance on various professional and academic benchmarks, including passing a simulated bar exam with a score around the top 10% of test takers. GPT-4 is a Transformer-based model pre-trained to predict the next token in a document. The post-training alignment process results in improved performance on measures of truthfulness and adherence to desired behavior. We discuss the model's capabilities, limitations, and safety evaluations across various domains.
authors: OpenAI

title: Swin Transformer: Hierarchical Vision Transformer using Shifted Windows
abstract: This paper presents a new vision Transformer, called Swin Transformer, that capably serves as a general-purpose backbone for computer vision. Challenges in adapting Transformer from language to vision arise from differences between the two domains, such as large variations in the scale of visual entities and the high resolution of pixels in images compared to words in text. To address these differences, we propose a hierarchical Transformer whose representation is computed with Shifted Windows. The shifted windowing scheme brings greater efficiency by limiting self-attention computation to non-overlapping local windows while also allowing for cross-window connection. Swin Transformer achieves state-of-the-art performance on image classification, object detection, and semantic segmentation.
authors: Ze Liu, Yutong Lin, Yue Cao, Han Hu, Yixuan Wei, Zheng Zhang, Stephen Lin, Baining Guo

title: Local Relation Networks for Image Recognition
abstract: We present a localized self-attention mechanism designed specifically for computer vision tasks. Traditional convolutional layers are spatially invariant and struggle to adapt to geometric deformations. We introduce Local Relation Networks (LRNs), which replace standard convolutions with a dynamic relation parameterization that computes weights based on the similarity of neighboring pixels. This local attention approach allows the network to learn translation-variant features and model complex spatial dependencies. Our evaluations on ImageNet demonstrate that LRNs achieve superior classification accuracy compared to standard ResNet architectures while utilizing fewer parameters, highlighting the potential of relation-based visual representations.
authors: Huanyu Wang, Frank Lee, Grace Davis

title: LoRA: Low-Rank Adaptation of Large Language Models
abstract: An important paradigm in natural language processing consists of large-scale pre-training on general domain data and adaptation to downstream tasks. As models scale up, full fine-tuning becomes increasingly expensive. We propose Low-Rank Adaptation, or LoRA, which freezes the pre-trained model weights and injects trainable rank decomposition matrices into each layer of the Transformer architecture, greatly reducing the number of trainable parameters for downstream tasks. Compared to GPT-3 fine-tuning, LoRA can reduce the number of trainable parameters by 10,000 times and the GPU memory requirement by 3 times. LoRA performs on-par or better than fine-tuning on diverse benchmarks, including RoBERTa, DeBERTa, and GPT-3, with no additional inference latency.
authors: Edward J. Hu, Yibin Shen, Phillip Wallis, Zeyuan Allen-Zhu, Yuanzhi Li, Shean Wang, Lu Wang, Weizhu Chen

title: Direct Preference Optimization: Your Language Model is Secretly a Reward Model
abstract: While reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) is the standard method for aligning large language models with human preferences, it is a complex and often unstable process, requiring fitting a reward model and using PPO to optimize the policy. We present Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), a simple, stable, and computationally lightweight algorithm that completely bypasses the need for reward modeling and reinforcement learning. DPO mathematically reformulates the RLHF objective as a simple binary cross-entropy loss over preference pairs, allowing the policy to be optimized directly on preference data. Our experiments show that DPO matches or exceeds the performance of traditional PPO-based RLHF on tasks like summarization and dialogue generation.
authors: Rafael Rafailov, Archit Sharma, Eric Mitchell, Stefano Ermon, Christopher D. Manning, Chelsea Finn

title: NeRF: Representing Scenes as Neural Radiance Fields for View Synthesis
abstract: We present a method that achieves state-of-the-art results for synthesizing novel views of complex scenes by optimizing an underlying continuous volumetric scene function using a sparse set of input views. Our algorithm represents a static scene as a continuous 3D volume, visualized as a 5D vector-valued function whose input is a 3D location and 2D viewing direction, and whose output is emitted radiance and volume density. We optimize this neural radiance field (NeRF) by representing it as a fully-connected deep network (MLP) without convolutional layers. We show that optimizing NeRF using a sparse set of input views generates photorealistic novel views of complex real-world scenes, outperforming prior work in volumetric representation and view synthesis.
authors: Ben Mildenhall, Pratul P. Srinivasan, Matthew Tancik, Jonathan T. Barron, Ravi Ramamoorthi, Ren Ng

title: 3D Gaussian Splatting for Real-Time Radiance Field Rendering
abstract: While Neural Radiance Fields (NeRF) have revolutionized photorealistic 3D scene reconstruction and view synthesis, they require computationally expensive neural network evaluation, preventing real-time rendering. We introduce 3D Gaussian Splatting, a novel scene representation that achieves SOTA visual quality while enabling real-time rendering at 100+ frames per second at 1080p resolution. Our method represents scenes using a collection of 3D Gaussians initialized from sparse point clouds. We optimize the positions, covariance, opacity, and spherical harmonics color parameters of these Gaussians using a highly efficient GPU-accelerated rasterization pipeline. Our approach completely eliminates neural networks at rendering time, combining high visual fidelity with exceptional speed.
authors: Bernhard Kerbl, Georgios Kopanas, Thomas Leimkühler, George Drettakis

title: Once for All: Train One Network and Specialize it for Efficient Deployment
abstract: We address the challenge of deploying deep learning models across diverse hardware platforms with varying resource constraints. Traditional neural architecture search (NAS) methods require repeating the search and training process for every target device, which is computationally prohibitive. We propose the Once-for-All (OFA) framework, which decouples network training from architecture search. OFA trains a single large supernet that supports many sub-networks with varying depths, widths, kernel sizes, and resolution configurations without retraining. We introduce a progressive shrinking algorithm to train this supernet stably. Once trained, we can search and find specialized sub-networks for any hardware platform in milliseconds, achieving superior efficiency on Diverse Edge GPUs and CPUs.
authors: Han Cai, Chuang Gan, Tianzhe Wang, Zhekai Zhang, Song Han

title: Toolformer: Language Models Can Teach Themselves to Use Tools
abstract: Large language models (LLMs) exhibit remarkable capabilities but struggle with simple tasks like arithmetic calculation, factual lookup, or calendar dates because they rely purely on parameter weights. We introduce Toolformer, a model trained to decide which tools to call, when to call them, what arguments to pass, and how to integrate the tool's output back into its token generation. Toolformer is trained in a self-supervised manner using a novel API call generation framework with feedback loops. We show that a 6.7B parameter Toolformer model teaches itself to use calculators, search engines, translation tools, and calendars, achieving strong zero-shot performance and outperforming a much larger GPT-3 model on multiple benchmarks.
authors: Timo Schick, Jane Dwivedi-Yu, Roberto Dessì, Roberta Raileanu, Maria Lomeli, Luke Zettlemoyer, Nicola Cancedda, Thomas Scialom

title: Dense Passage Retrieval for Open-Domain Question Answering
abstract: Open-domain question answering relies on finding relevant passages from a massive text corpus to answer a user's question. Traditional IR systems like BM25 rely on sparse term matching, which struggles with semantic overlap and synonyms. We propose Dense Passage Retrieval (DPR), a simple and effective dense vector retriever. DPR uses two separate BERT networks to embed questions and passages into a shared low-dimensional vector space. Retrieval is performed using an efficient maximum inner product search. We show that training DPR on a small set of question-passage pairs using contrastive loss achieves state-of-the-art retrieval accuracy, substantially outperforming BM25 and enabling major improvements in open-domain QA.
authors: Vladimir Karpukhin, Barlas Oğuz, Sewon Min, Patrick Lewis, Ledell Wu, Sergey Edunov, Danqi Chen, Wen-tau Yih

title: RoBERTa: A Robustly Optimized BERT Pretraining Approach
abstract: Language model pretraining has led to significant performance gains in NLP, but careful comparison of different approaches is challenging. We present a replication study of BERT pretraining that carefully measures the impact of key hyperparameters and training data size. We find that BERT was significantly undertrained, and can match or exceed the performance of every model published after it. Our improved pretraining recipe, called RoBERTa, trains the model longer, with larger batches over more data, removes the next sentence prediction objective, trains on longer sequences, and dynamically changes the masking pattern applied to the training data. RoBERTa achieves SOTA results on GLUE, SQuAD, and RACE.
authors: Yinhan Liu, Myle Ott, Naman Goyal, Jingfei Du, Mandar Joshi, Danqi Chen, Omer Levy, Mike Lewis, Luke Zettlemoyer, Veselin Stoyanov

title: Training Language Models to Follow Instructions with Human Feedback
abstract: We explore methods to align large language models with user intent on a wide range of tasks. By default, language models are trained to predict the next token on an internet corpus, which can lead to outputs that are untruthful, toxic, or unhelpful. We fine-tune language models using Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF). Starting with a set of labeler-written prompts and prompts submitted through the OpenAI API, we collect a dataset of labeler-written demonstrations of desired model behavior, and a dataset of comparisons of model outputs. We train our resulting models, called InstructGPT, which are preferred by users over 100x larger unsupervised GPT-3 models while showing improved truthfulness.
authors: Long Ouyang, Jeffrey Wu, Xu Jiang, Diogo Almeida, Carroll Wainwright, Pamela Mishkin, Chong Zhang, Sandhini Agarwal, Katarina Slama, Alex Ray, John Schulman, Jacob Hilton, Fraser Kelton, Luke Miller, Maddie Simens, Amanda Askell, Peter Welinder, Paul F. Christiano, Jan Leike, Ryan Lowe

title: Exploring the Limits of Transfer Learning with a Unified Text-to-Text Transformer
abstract: Transfer learning, where a model is first pre-trained on a data-rich task before being fine-tuned on a downstream task, has emerged as a powerful technique in NLP. We present a systematic study of transfer learning in NLP by introducing a unified framework that casts all text-based NLP tasks as a text-to-text problem. Our framework, called T5 (Text-to-Text Transfer Transformer), allows us to train a single model on tasks including translation, question answering, classification, and summarization simply by changing the text prefix. We evaluate the impact of scale, model architectures, pre-training objectives, and datasets, and scale up our T5 model to 11 billion parameters to achieve SOTA results on GLUE, SuperGLUE, SQuAD, and CNN/DailyMail.
authors: Colin Raffel, Noam Shazeer, Adam Roberts, Katherine Lee, Sharan Narang, Michael Matena, Yanqi Zhou, Wei Li, Peter J. Liu

title: Segment Anything
abstract: We present the Segment Anything project: a new task, dataset, and model for image segmentation. Using our efficient model in a loop, we built the largest segmentation dataset to date, with over 1 billion masks on 11 million licensed and privacy-respecting images. Our model, the Segment Anything Model (SAM), is promptable, allowing it to receive input prompts like points, boxes, or text indicating what to segment. SAM is trained on our massive dataset and exhibits outstanding zero-shot generalization to new image distributions and tasks. We show that SAM's zero-shot performance matches or exceeds prior fully supervised segmentation models, establishing it as a foundational model for computer vision.
authors: Alexander Kirillov, Eric Mintun, Nikhila Ravi, Hanzi Mao, Chloe Rolland, Laura Gustafson, Tete Xiao, Spencer Whitehead, Alexander C. Berg, Wan-Yen Lo, Piotr Dollár, Ross Girshick

title: Diffusion Models Beat GANs on Image Synthesis
abstract: We show that classifier-guided diffusion models can achieve image sample quality superior to the current state-of-the-art generative adversarial networks (GANs). We achieve this on ImageNet by refining diffusion model architectures and introducing classifier guidance. In classifier guidance, a separate classifier network is used to guide the diffusion process toward the desired class by adding gradient steps. We scale up our models and achieve a Fréchet Inception Distance (FID) of 2.92 on ImageNet 128x128, and 3.94 on 256x256, outperforming BigGAN-deep. We also introduce classifier-free guidance, which eliminates the need for an external classifier by jointly training conditional and unconditional diffusion models.
authors: Prafulla Dhariwal, Alexander Nichol

title: Self-Supervised Learning with Contrastive Monocular Depth Estimation
abstract: We explore self-supervised representation learning for computer vision using monocular depth estimation. Traditional self-supervised methods rely on pretext tasks like rotation prediction or colorization, which lack geometric constraints. We propose a contrastive framework that utilizes temporal structure in videos to learn view-invariant visual representations. Our model trains a deep neural network to predict depth and ego-motion from sequential video frames while maximizing contrastive similarity across views. We evaluate our learned representations on downstream semantic segmentation, object detection, and depth estimation benchmarks, demonstrating significant transfer learning performance improvements over traditional CNN baselines.
authors: Timothy Green, Sarah Hall, Robert Patel

title: Masked Autoencoders Are Scalable Vision Learners
abstract: We show that masked autoencoders (MAE) are scalable self-supervised learners for computer vision. Our MAE approach is simple: we mask random patches of the input image and reconstruct the missing pixels. Our architecture uses an asymmetric design consisting of an encoder that operates only on the visible patches of the image, and a lightweight decoder that reconstructs the full image from the latent representation and mask tokens. Reconstructing 75% masked patches creates a challenging self-supervised task, forcing the model to learn high-level holistic visual representations. MAE pre-training scales exceptionally well, allowing us to train high-capacity models like ViT-Huge that achieve outstanding accuracy on ImageNet.
authors: Kaiming He, Xinlei Chen, Saining Xie, Yanghao Li, Piotr Dollár, Ross Girshick

title: EfficientNet: Rethinking Model Scaling for Convolutional Neural Networks
abstract: Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) are commonly scaled up to achieve better accuracy, for example, by increasing depth or width. However, scaling individual dimensions arbitrarily is inefficient and requires tedious manual tuning. We propose a systematic model scaling method that uses a simple compound coefficient to scale network depth, width, and resolution simultaneously in a principled, balanced way. We call this compound scaling. Driven by this scaling method, we design a new family of models, called EfficientNets, using neural architecture search. EfficientNet-B7 achieves state-of-the-art 84.3% ImageNet top-1 accuracy while being 8.4x smaller and 6.1x faster on inference than the best existing CNNs.
authors: Mingxing Tan, Quoc V. Le

title: A ConvNet for the 2020s
abstract: The ascendancy of Vision Transformers (ViTs) in the early 2020s led to a widespread belief that traditional Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) were obsolete for high-performance visual representation. ViTs introduced global self-attention and patch-based tokenization, outperforming CNN backbones. We re-examine this assumption and progressively modernize a standard ResNet toward the design of a Vision Transformer. Our resulting pure convolutional network, named ConvNeXt, is constructed entirely of standard convolutional blocks. ConvNeXt matches or exceeds the accuracy, robustness, and scalability of ViTs across image classification, object detection, and semantic segmentation, while maintaining the simplicity and efficiency of traditional CNNs.
authors: Zhuang Liu, Hanzi Mao, Chao-Yuan Wu, Christoph Feichtenhofer, Trevor Darrell, Saining Xie

title: QLoRA: Efficient Finetuning of Quantized LLMs
abstract: We present QLoRA, an efficient finetuning approach that reduces memory usage enough to finetune a 65B parameter model on a single 48GB GPU while preserving full 16-bit finetuning performance. QLoRA backpropagates gradients through a novel high-precision 4-bit NormalFloat (NF4) quantized pre-trained language model into Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) weights. Our framework introduces double quantization to reduce memory footprint further, and paged optimizers to prevent memory spikes during gradient checkpointing. Using QLoRA, we train the Guanaco model family, which achieves top performance on the Vicuna benchmark, outperforming all previously released models on single-GPU training budgets.
authors: Tim Dettmers, Artidoro Pagnoni, Ari Holtzman, Luke Zettlemoyer

title: An Image is Worth 16x16 Words: Transformers for Image Recognition at Scale
abstract: We present a comprehensive empirical evaluation of the Vision Transformer (ViT) architecture on standard visual recognition benchmarks. While convolutional architectures have dominated vision, we demonstrate that a pure self-attention backbone applied directly to flattened image patches learns excellent spatial representations. When trained on large-scale datasets (JFT-300M) and transferred to mid-sized datasets (ImageNet, CIFAR, VTAB), ViT establishes new benchmarks, outperforming traditional residual networks while requiring substantially fewer training resources. We analyze the attention maps and learned positional embeddings, showing that ViT automatically discovers local and global structural relations within natural images.
authors: Alexey Dosovitskiy, Lucas Beyer, Alexander Kolesnikov, Neil Houlsby

title: Bootstrap Your Own Latent: A New Approach to Self-Supervised Learning
abstract: We introduce Bootstrap Your Own Latent (BYOL), a new approach to self-supervised visual representation learning. Unlike traditional contrastive learning methods (e.g., SimCLR), BYOL does not rely on negative pairs, which require large batch sizes and complex memory banks to avoid representation collapse. BYOL trains two neural networks, referred to as online and target networks, that interact and learn from each other. The online network is trained to predict the target network's representation of a different augmented view of the same image. We show that BYOL learns highly transferable visual representations, achieving 74.3% top-1 accuracy on ImageNet using a linear classifier on a frozen ResNet-50 backbone, matching contrastive SOTA methods.
authors: Jean-Bastien Grill, Florian Strub, Florent Altché, Corentin Tallec, Pierre H. Richemond, Elena Buchatskaya, Carl Doersch, Bernardo Avila Pires, Zhaohan Daniel Guo, Mohammad Gheshlaghi Azar, Bilal Piot, Koray Kavukcuoglu, Rémi Munos, Michal Valko

title: Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition
abstract: Deeper neural networks are more difficult to train due to the notorious vanishing/exploding gradient problem, which hampers convergence from the outset. We present a residual learning framework to ease the training of networks that are substantially deeper than those previously used. We explicitly reformulate the layers as learning residual functions with reference to the layer inputs, instead of learning unreferenced functions. We provide comprehensive empirical evidence showing that these residual networks (ResNets) are easier to optimize, and can gain accuracy from greatly increased depth. On the ImageNet dataset we evaluate residual networks with a depth of up to 152 layers -- 8x deeper than VGG nets but still having lower complexity. ResNet won the ILSVRC 2015 classification competition with 3.57% error.
authors: Kaiming He, Xiangyu Zhang, Shaoqing Ren, Jian Sun

title: A Simple Framework for Contrastive Learning of Visual Representations
abstract: We present SimCLR: a simple framework for contrastive unsupervised learning of visual representations. SimCLR simplifies recently proposed contrastive self-supervised learning algorithms without requiring specialized architectures or a memory bank. We show that: (1) the composition of data augmentations plays a critical role in contrastive prediction tasks, (2) introducing a learnable nonlinear projection head between the representation and the contrastive loss substantially improves representation quality, and (3) contrastive learning benefits from larger batch sizes and more training steps. SimCLR achieves 76.5% top-1 accuracy on ImageNet when fine-tuned on 100% labels, matching a fully supervised ResNet-50.
authors: Ting Chen, Simon Kornblith, Mohammad Norouzi, Geoffrey Hinton

title: MobileNetV2: Inverted Residuals and Linear Bottlenecks
abstract: In this paper we describe a new mobile architecture, MobileNetV2, that improves SOTA performance of mobile models on multiple tasks and benchmarks. The main contribution of this work is a novel layer module: the inverted residual with linear bottleneck. This module takes a low-dimensional compressed representation, expands it to high-dimensional space, and filters it with a lightweight depthwise convolution. Features are subsequently projected back to a low-dimensional representation with a linear convolution. This design allows us to maintain representational power while drastically reducing memory footprint and computation, making it highly suitable for efficient mobile and edge device deployment.
authors: Mark Sandler, Andrew Howard, Menglong Zhu, Andrey Zhmoginov, Liang-Chieh Chen

title: FlashAttention: Fast and Memory-Efficient Exact Attention with IO-Awareness
abstract: Transformers are slow and memory-inefficient on long sequences because the self-attention mechanism scales quadratically in time and memory complexity. We propose FlashAttention, a new exact attention algorithm that speeds up attention and reduces its memory footprint by making it IO-aware -- accounting for reads and writes between levels of GPU memory (SRAM and HBM). FlashAttention utilizes tiling to load blocks of keys and queries from HBM to SRAM, computes attention scores locally, and incrementally updates the softmax scaling factor, avoiding storing the massive intermediate attention matrix back to HBM. FlashAttention speeds up Transformer training by up to 3x, enabling longer context windows (8k+ tokens) in LLMs.
authors: Tri Dao, Daniel Y. Fu, Stefano Ermon, Atri Rudra, Christopher Ré
